description: ETA was an armed Basque nationalist and separatist organization active in Spain and France between 1959 and 2018. Founded in 1959 during Francoist Spain by Basque nationalist students, it evolved into a clandestine paramilitary group conducting assassinations, bombings, and kidnappings primarily in Spain. ETA killed over 800 people and was classified as a terrorist group by multiple countries. It announced a definitive end to armed activity in 2011 and formally dissolved in 2018.

country_links.evidence_quote: Following a judicial investigation, it was reported that FARC and ETA had held meetings in Colombia, exchanging information about combat tactics...
country_links.context: ETA members received training from FARC in Colombia and held meetings with FARC leaders there.

country_links.evidence_quote: Several ex-militants were sent from France through Panama to reside in Cuba after an agreement of the Spanish government (under Felipe González) with Cuba.
country_links.context: Several ex-militants were sent from France through Panama to reside in Cuba after an agreement with the Spanish government.

country_links.evidence_quote: Actions in France usually consisted of assaults on arsenals or military industries to steal weapons or explosives; these were usually stored in large quantities in hide-outs located in the French Basque Country rather than Spain.
country_links.context: ETA operated in France, conducting assaults on arsenals, storing weapons, and engaging in shootings with French gendarmerie.

country_links.evidence_quote: former Taoiseach of Ireland Bertie Ahern
country_links.context: ETA's ceasefire declaration was supported by former Taoiseach Bertie Ahern and Sinn Féin president Gerry Adams, indicating Irish involvement in peace efforts.

country_links.evidence_quote: ETA moved the extorted funds to accounts in Liechtenstein and other fiscal havens.
country_links.context: ETA moved extorted funds to accounts in Liechtenstein.

country_links.evidence_quote: Islamic fundamentalists from Morocco were eventually convicted
country_links.context: Islamic fundamentalists from Morocco were convicted for the 2004 Madrid bombings, but ETA was initially blamed.

country_links.evidence_quote: former prime minister of Norway Gro Harlem Brundtland
country_links.context: ETA's ceasefire declaration was supported by former prime minister of Norway Gro Harlem Brundtland.

country_links.evidence_quote: ETA exchanged weapons, explosives and provided logistical support to the Forças Populares 25 de Abril (FP-25), a Portuguese far-left terrorist group.
country_links.context: ETA exchanged weapons, explosives, and provided logistical support to the Portuguese group FP-25.

country_links.evidence_quote: former UK Prime Minister Tony Blair
country_links.context: ETA's ceasefire declaration was supported by former UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and British diplomat Jonathan Powell.

country_links.evidence_quote: resulting in one death (American citizen Eugene Kent Brown, employee of Johnson & Johnson)
country_links.context: ETA killed an American citizen in a car bomb attack in Madrid.
